scenarios.task: Our day spa has 6 treatment rooms and utilization is running at about 61%. The owner wants to hire 2 more massage therapists to grow revenue. Is that the right move?
criteria: Says the room-hour ceiling, not therapist headcount, is the actual capacity constraint, and hiring won't raise it
Recommends first checking whether the utilization gap is a scheduling/menu-duration fragmentation problem versus a genuine demand problem before hiring
Explicitly recommends against the 2 new hires as the first move, or ties any hiring recommendation to utilization already being near the practical ceiling (roughly 80-85%)

scenarios.task: One of our massage therapists works exclusively at our spa, on a schedule we set, using our rooms and equipment, and charges the prices we set on our menu. We currently classify her as a 1099 independent contractor to save on payroll tax. Any concerns?
criteria: Flags this as a likely worker misclassification issue based on the behavioral/financial control factors described (schedule, equipment, pricing all controlled by the spa)
States that classification is a legal test based on actual working conditions, not a cost-optimization choice or what the contract label says
Warns about back-tax/penalty exposure rather than treating the current setup as low-risk
